candidate_problems.text: Is computational irreducibility actually the load-bearing cause of persistent capture, or can much of the phenomenon be explained by simpler mechanisms such as incentive misalignment, principal-agent failure, concentrated benefits/diffuse costs, and political bargaining? This matters because if capture is only partly irreducible, then better models, narrower scope, modular regulation, or incentive redesign may outperform the conjecture’s emphasis on permanent adversarial iteration.

Under what conditions can institutions escape or substantially blunt the asymmetry that regulated actors are better positioned than regulators to compute rule consequences? The open question is whether there exist structural designs—e.g., independent data generation, prediction markets, red-team regulators, randomized rule variation, open technical standards, or distributed oversight—that change the game qualitatively rather than merely shifting capture into new channels.

How can the conjecture be operationalized and tested empirically without collapsing into an unfalsifiable claim that every reform failure is just adaptation? A key unresolved tension is identifying observable signatures that distinguish 'irreducible adaptive capture' from ordinary policy drift or corruption—for example, measurable migration of influence across channels after reforms, rising procedural complexity with stable outcomes, or persistent dependence on industry epistemic inputs.
